perturbation.original_text: 12. GENERAL 12.1. Assignment and other dealings. Neither Party shall assign, transfer, subcontract or deal in any other manner with any of its rights and obligations under this Agreement without the prior written agreement of the other Party .
perturbation.changed_text: 2.2. Confidential Information does not include information which: a) which is or subsequently becomes public knowledge or publicly available through no faul t of the Receiving Party; or b) which is known by the Receiving Party prior to the time of receipt, as evidenced by its written records; or c) which is lawfully obtained by the Receiving Party from a third party and is not subject to a similar restriction on dis closure; d) which is independently developed by Receiving Party; or e) the Parties agree in writing is not confidential. Neither Party shall assign, transfer, subcontract or deal in any other manner with any of its rights and obligations under this Agreement without the prior written agreement of the other Party.
perturbation.explanation: The 'Assignment' clause restricts a party's ability to transfer its obligations. Moving this clause to the 'Confidential Information' definition section makes it less visible and harder to find, potentially leading a party to unknowingly violate the restriction.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Restatement (Second) of Contracts § 317 deals with the assignment of rights. Moving a no-assignment clause into a definition section could lead a party to believe the clause is merely informational, rather than a binding restriction on their right to assign the contract. This obscurity can lead to disputes about whether the assignment restriction was properly communicated and agreed upon.
perturbation.justification: The original contract's 'Assignment' clause ensures that neither party can transfer their obligations without written consent. The Structural Flaws - Legal Contradiction perturbation type applies here because moving the 'Assignment' clause to the 'Confidential Information' section weakens its visibility and enforceability. While the scraped law snippets are empty, the Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation highlight that this relocation could mislead a party into thinking the clause is merely informational rather than a binding restriction, potentially resulting in disputes about the proper communication of assignment restrictions. This aligns with the definition of Structural Flaws Legal Contradiction, where a required legal disclosure is placed in an irrelevant or misleading section, making it difficult for parties to locate or interpret, which would lead to the weakening of the legal obligation. Therefore, the modification does create a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 4. EXCEPTIONS 4.1. Notwithstanding clause 3.1 of this Agreement, the Receiving Party may disclose the Confidential Information : a) to its and its Affiliate’s Representatives , provided always that the Receiving Party procures that such Representatives shall comply with the confidentiality obligations as set out in clause 3 of this Agreement , and the Receiving P arty agrees to be liable for the actions or omissions of such Representatives in relation to the Confidential Information as if they were the actions or omissions of the Receiving Party ; and b) as may be required by an order of any court of competent jurisdic tion or governmental body in which case t he Receiving Party shall, to the extent permitted by law, use reasonable endeavours to provide the Disclosing Party with prompt written notice of any such requirement prior to any disclosure so that the Disclosing P arty may seek a protection order or other appropriate remedy. If no protective order or remedy is obtained by the Disclosing Party, the Receiving Party shall disclose only that portion of the Confidential Information that it is legally required to disclose .
perturbation.changed_text: 1. DEFINITIONS Affiliate : shall mean any other corporation or other form of business entity, which, directly or indirectly, controls, is controlled by, or is under common control with, a Party at any time during the term of this Agreement. For purposes of this definition, the term “control” means direct or indirect beneficial ownership of more than 50% of the voting share or interest of the entity; Confidential Information : has the meaning given in clause 2 of this Agreement; Disclosing Party : a Party to this Agreement which discloses its Confidential Information to the other Party; Purpose : [[INSERT DETAILS e.g. to establish a business relationship in respect to the supply of [named service provision or goods] from X, etc]]; Receiving Party : a Party to this Agreement which receives the Confidential Information from the other P arty; Representatives : any employees, officers, directors, professional advisors or consultants in relation to each Party and any of its Affiliates, who are actively and directly engaged in the Purpose; Term : has the meaning given in clause 10.1 of this Agreement. Notwithstanding clause 3.1 of this Agreement, the Receiving Party may disclose the Confidential Information : a) to its and its Affiliate’s Representatives , provided always that the Receiving Party procures that such Representatives shall comply with the confidentiality obligations as set out in clause 3 of this Agreement , and the Receiving P arty agrees to be liable for the actions or omissions of such Representatives in relation to the Confidential Information as if they were the actions or omissions of the Receiving Party ; and b) as may be required by an order of any court of competent jurisdic tion or governmental body in which case t he Receiving Party shall, to the extent permitted by law, use reasonable endeavours to provide the Disclosing Party with prompt written notice of any such requirement prior to any disclosure so that the Disclosing P arty may seek a protection order or other appropriate remedy. If no protective order or remedy is obtained by the Disclosing Party, the Receiving Party shall disclose only that portion of the Confidential Information that it is legally required to disclose .
perturbation.explanation: The Exceptions clause provides crucial exemptions to the confidentiality obligations. Moving it to the Definitions section, while technically present, buries it within definitional text, making it less noticeable and potentially overlooked by someone reviewing the agreement for operational implications. This is against the core intent of drawing someone's attention to these exemptions to confidentiality. It changes the relative visibility of the clause.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Contract Law - Duty of Good Faith and Fair Dealing
perturbation.law_citation: UCC § 1-304 - Although the contract is under the laws of England and Wales, the principle of good faith applies in most jurisdictions
perturbation.law_explanation: While the UK has no codified duty of good faith like the US UCC § 1-304, the principle of acting honestly is implied in contracts. Hiding a crucial 'Exceptions' clause within the 'Definitions' section arguably violates this principle by making it difficult for the other party to understand their rights and obligations, which leads to a case of potentially bad faith during the contract's performance. The location affects reasonable interpretation.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 1-304. Obligation of Good Faith. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 1-304. Obligation of Good Faith. Every contract or duty within the Uniform Commercial Code imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The perturbation involves a structural flaw by moving the 'Exceptions' clause to the 'Definitions' section. This placement obscures the exceptions to confidentiality, potentially leading to a party overlooking these exceptions when reviewing the contract for operational implications. The Law Explanation argues this could violate the principle of good faith. Although Scraped Law Snippet 2 is empty, Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing UCC § 1-304, emphasizes the 'obligation of good faith' in contract performance. While this snippet refers to the US UCC, the Law Explanation extends a similar principle to UK contracts. By burying the exception clause, the modified contract arguably undermines the principle of good faith by making it harder for the other party to understand their obligations. This supports a 'YES' determination.

perturbation.original_text: 12.8. Governing Law. This Agreement and any dispute or claim (including non -contractual disputes or claims) arising out of or in connection with it or its subject matter or formation shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of England and Wales. 12.9. Jurisdiction. Each Party irrevocable agrees that the courts of England and Wales shall have exc lusive jurisdiction to settle any dispute or claim (including non -contractual disputes or claims) arising out of or in connection with this Agreement or its subject matter or formation.
perturbation.changed_text: 7. NO REPRESENTATION 7.1. Neither of the Parties make any express or implied representation or warranty as to the accuracy , reliability or completeness of the Confidential Information, expressly disclaiming any and all liability that may be based on the Confidential Information, including any errors therein or omissions there from. 7.2. The Parties further agree that they are not entitled to rely on the accuracy or completeness of the Confidential Information. This Agreement and any dispute or claim (including non -contractual disputes or claims) arising out of or in connection with it or its subject matter or formation shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of England and Wales. Each Party irrevocable agrees that the courts of England and Wales shall have exc lusive jurisdiction to settle any dispute or claim (including non -contractual disputes or claims) arising out of or in connection with this Agreement or its subject matter or formation.
perturbation.explanation: Moving the 'Governing Law and Jurisdiction' clause to the 'No Representation' section makes it less prominent and harder to find. This could lead a party to overlook the specific jurisdiction and governing law applicable to the contract. By embedding it here, it is less likely that it is adequately understood.
perturbation.law_citation: Rome I Regulation - Although Governing Law is permitted to be placed in the contract, hiding this conflicts with reasonable notice requirements
perturbation.law_explanation: While the Rome I Regulation permits parties to choose the governing law, moving the governing law clause to the 'No Representation' section arguably conflicts with the principle of reasonable notice. By burying it in a less visible and relevant section, it could be argued that the other party did not have adequate notice of the governing law and jurisdiction, potentially making it unenforceable.

perturbation.justification: The original contract has the governing law and jurisdiction in section 12. The modified contract moves this clause to section 7, titled 'No Representation'. The Law Explanation suggests that this placement could be argued as not providing adequate notice of the governing law and jurisdiction. The Perturbation Explanation also indicates that this change makes it less prominent, harder to find, and less likely to be adequately understood, fulfilling the 'Structural Flaws Legal Contradiction' definition, because it places the governing law in an irrelevant section, making it difficult for parties to locate or interpret.
